era_context: The Neo-Assyrian Empire under Sargon II continues its aggressive expansion and consolidation. In the Levant, the aftermath of the siege of Samaria (which began in 722 BCE) is ongoing. In Greece, the Archaic period continues with the development of city-states and early colonization. In China, the Spring and Autumn period is in its early phase, with the Zhou dynasty's authority fragmenting.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Greece (limited specific events for this year)
China (limited synchronization with Old World chronologies)
Mesoamerica (no synchronization with Old World calendars)

events.title: Sargon II's Continued Military Campaigns in Anatolia and the North
events.description: Sargon II continues his military campaigns in Anatolia and against northern frontier threats. In 706 BCE, he is engaged in consolidating Assyrian control over the Anatolian highlands and suppressing rebellions in vassal states. These campaigns are part of his broader strategy to secure the empire's northern and eastern borders. The exact details of campaigns in this specific year are poorly documented in surviving sources.

sources.name: Sargon II's Annals (fragmentary for this year)

events.certainty_note: Sargon II's annals are not uniformly detailed for every year. The year 706 BCE falls within his reign (722–705 BCE), but specific events for this year are not clearly attested in surviving sources. This entry represents the broader pattern of his military activity.

events.title: Samaria Siege Continues (Year 17 of Hoshea's Reign)
events.description: The siege of Samaria, which began in 722 BCE under Sargon II, continues into its later stages. According to biblical and Assyrian sources, the siege lasted three years (722–720 BCE). By 706 BCE, the siege has already concluded (in 720 BCE), and the city has been captured and repopulated. This entry reflects the ongoing consolidation of Assyrian control in the aftermath of the siege.
events.certainty_note: The siege of Samaria is dated to 722–720 BCE. By 706 BCE, the siege has concluded. This entry is included to clarify the timeline, though the major event (the fall of Samaria) occurred in 722 BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: The year 706 BCE is poorly documented in surviving sources. No specific major events are clearly attested for this year in Mesopotamia, the Levant, or elsewhere. The entries above represent inferences from broader patterns of Sargon II's reign rather than documented events for this specific year. This is a limitation of the historical record, not a disconfirmation of events listed.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 706 BCE is sparse. Assyrian annals are the primary source for this period, but they do not provide uniform coverage of every year. The biblical record is retrospective and selective. Archaeological evidence is limited to material remains from sites like Samaria, which do not provide year-by-year chronological precision. For most regions outside the Assyrian sphere, there are no contemporary written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: Sargon II's northern campaigns continue until his death in 705 BCE
